C:
Use a monorepo. For a 20-engineer startup, it's the right default, and the case for splitting into many repos mostly applies to problems you don't have yet.

Here's the reasoning.

**Why monorepo fits your stage**

At 20 engineers you're still small enough that the whole company often needs to move together — change an API and its three consumers in one pull request, do an atomic refactor across services, share types and utilities without publishing internal packages. A monorepo makes that a single, reviewable, revertible change. Multi-repo turns the same edit into a choreography of version bumps, coordinated merges, and "which repo is on which version" debugging — overhead that scales badly with how often your interfaces are still changing, which at a startup is constantly.

You also get one source of truth for tooling: one linter config, one CI setup, one way to run tests, one dependency version policy. With many repos that config drifts, and keeping it in sync becomes its own recurring tax.

Onboarding is simpler too — clone one thing, see the whole system, search across all of it. New hires find code instead of asking which of 15 repos something lives in.

**The real costs, and why they're manageable now**

The honest downsides of monorepos are tooling and scale: naive CI that rebuilds everything on every commit gets slow, and git operations slow down at very large repo sizes. But both bite at hundreds of engineers and millions of lines, not 20 people. You can defer the heavy machinery (Bazel, Nx, Turborepo) until you actually feel the pain, and adopt selective/affected-only CI when builds start dragging. Starting simple is fine.

Access control is the one genuine limitation: a monorepo gives everyone visibility into everything. For most 20-person startups that's a feature, not a problem. If you have a hard need to wall off a codebase (a security-sensitive component, or external contractors who must only see one slice), that specific thing can live in its own repo. That's the main condition under which I'd carve out an exception — not the default.

**When I'd reconsider**

I'd flip to multi-repo only if you have genuinely independent products with separate teams, release cadences, and almost no shared code — or that access-control requirement above. Neither is typical at 20 engineers building toward a shared product.

**Recommendation:** Start with a single monorepo, conventional folder structure (e.g. `apps/`, `packages/` or `services/`, `libs/`), standard CI to begin with, and add affected-only build tooling when CI time becomes a real complaint. Carve out a separate repo only for the rare thing that truly needs isolation.
